title: Evals manual: grounding criteria guidance for context-receiving skills
description: Decision from reasoning-trap research review (docs/superpowers/research/2026-06-11-reasoning-trap.md, point 3a). Add a 'grounding criteria' subsection to the evals manual rubric guidance: skills whose output reasons over provided material (RAG, summarisation, analysis, classification-with-rationale) must include binary grounding criteria — 'every factual claim is supported by the provided context', 'quotes/cites the source span for key claims', negative form 'does not introduce facts absent from the context'. Rationale: accuracy-only evals are blind to grounding decay (Reasoning Trap: 88% accuracy retained with 43% faithfulness loss). Scope limit: only where context exists; creative skills skip. Doubles as verbosity-bias hardening.
close_reason: Grounding-criteria section added to docs/evals.md (binary supported-claims/cited-spans/no-invented-facts criteria for context-receiving skills, creative skills exempt, verbosity-hardening note) + at-a-glance rule 6.

title: Manifest graph support: recursive-CTE traversal + edge entity (GraphRAG prereq)
description: Part 2 (manifest-side; lower priority, only blocks the GraphRAG path). Edge entity = a self-join table (edgeSrc/edgeDst/edgeType/edgeMeta jsonb) using the existing self-FK pattern -- no new storage primitive. The one real addition: a withRecursive combinator beside withCte that emits WITH RECURSIVE (anchor UNION ALL step), reusing the qsWith/CteRef plumbing, plus neighborhood/reachable helpers with a depth+visited guard. Out of scope: Leiden community detection (algorithm, not SQL -> Haskell or extension) and true ANN (pgvector); brute-force cosine over jsonb is sufficient to ~10k. Clustering, when added, mints a typed Community layer (Community entity + community_member edges). Ref: shaping doc Part 2 + section 5.
notes: Implementation lands in the manifest repo, not crucible. Tracked here because the shaping doc and GraphRAG consumer live in crucible.

title: Session lifecycle hooks (onSessionStart / onSessionEnd)
description: ChatGPT Memory auto-injects recalled memories at session start and runs consolidation at session end (the 'dreaming' pass). Crucible has withMemories for manual injection and consolidate for explicit distillation, but no session lifecycle. Required: (1) onSessionStart :: (Memory :> es) => Query -> Instruction -> Eff es Instruction — recalls top-k memories matching a query and prepends them to the instruction preamble; (2) onSessionEnd :: (Memory :> es, LLM :> es) => Eff es () — runs consolidate over recent Episodic items and stamps results ByConsolidation. These can be thin wrappers over existing machinery.

title: Tool-callable memory interface
description: MemGPT exposes remember/recall/forget as LLM-callable tool functions so agents can manage their own memory. Crucible has the Memory effect and the Tool type but no bridge between them. Required: define Tool definitions for remember, recall, and forget that map to the Memory effect operations, and wire them into runToolAgentN so a tool-loop agent can page its own history to/from the JSONL store. This is separate from crucible-5yg (which covers the working-memory paging layer) — this issue is specifically the LLM-callable interface to the Memory effect.

title: withReflections combinator (Reflexion pattern)
description: Reflexion (Shinn et al., NeurIPS 2023) stores self-critiques after failed trials in a bounded FIFO episodic buffer and prepends them to the next trial context. Crucible has all the pieces but no combinator wiring them. Required: (1) reflectOnFailure :: Report -> Eff es MemoryDraft — generates a self-critique from a failed eval report and returns a MemoryDraft ready to remember; (2) withReflections :: (Memory :> es) => Int -> Skill i o -> Skill i o — recalls the last N episodic memory items and prepends them to the skill preamble before each run. Together these implement the Reflexion loop with no new storage machinery.

title: Embedding at write time for memory items
description: The Embed effect and cosine helper exist but are not called when writing to the memory store. Every remember should optionally embed the content and store the vector alongside the MemoryItem (e.g. embedding :: Maybe [Double]). This is the write-side prerequisite for crucible-try (cosine recall), Voyager-style skill retrieval, HuggingGPT-style tool routing by similarity, and AWM-style workflow retrieval by goal — all four are blocked on this. runMemoryFile should call embed at write time when an Embed interpreter is available in the effect stack.

title: Importance scoring at write time
description: MemoryItem has no importance field. Generative Agents and ChatGPT Memory both score importance at write time (LLM self-rates 1-10) and use it as a ranking signal in recall. Without this field, crucible-omw's weighted recall (recency + relevance + importance) cannot be completed. Required change: add importance :: Maybe Double to MemoryItem; in runMemoryFile (and optionally runMemoryPure), call the LLM once at remember to assign a score if the caller does not supply one. This is a prerequisite for crucible-omw.

title: Cross-session episodic recall with recency+importance+relevance ranking
description: The JSONL memory store persists across runs, so episodic memories survive sessions, but recall ranking is unweighted (most-recent-first up to maxItems budget). There is no Generative Agents-style retrieval scoring: recency (exponential decay) + importance (LLM-rated at write time) + relevance (cosine to query). Without importance scoring at write time and composite ranking at recall time, high-value episodic memories are crowded out by recency alone. This is the main gap between crucible's episodic store and production episodic memory systems.

title: Working memory / context window management
description: Conversation history is stateless — the Chat effect is caller-managed and there is no automatic paging or summarisation when the context window fills. Long-running tool-loop agents (runToolAgentN) have no mechanism to prune, summarise, or offload older turns to the Memory store. This gap means agents cannot handle extended multi-turn sessions without manual context trimming by the caller. A MemGPT-style approach would let the agent page turns to the JSONL memory store and inject summaries back into the working context.

title: Semantic retrieval from memory store
description: Recall is currently lexical only (case-folded infix needle + tag intersection). There is no embedding-indexed vector search over the memory store. The Embed effect and vector math (cosine) already exist — the gap is wiring them together at write time (embed content, store vector alongside MemoryItem) and at recall time (embed the query needle, rank by cosine similarity instead of or alongside lexical match). This would make Semantic MemoryKind items genuinely retrievable by meaning rather than keyword.

title: Test: autodocodec generic schema drops Maybe fields from required
description: SP3 final-review coverage gap. The migration weakened the schema-shape assertions to type==object/array checks; the old tests verified that optional (Maybe) record fields are dropped from a JSON-Schema's 'required' list. No current domain record uses a Maybe field through genericCodec, so this is latent — but before one is added, add a unit test asserting schemaValue of a record with a Maybe field yields a JSON-Schema whose 'required' omits that field (autodocodec's maybeCodec/optional handling). Surfaced reviewing feat/aeson-autodocodec.
close_reason: Test added and it exposed a real bug: Maybe fields were required on the wire. Fixed with an overlapping optionalFieldOrNullWith' selector instance; 3 tests pin required-list omission, absent-key decode, and explicit-null decode.

title: Streaming: guard against mid-stream idle timeout on long generations
description: The TLS manager's responseTimeout governs time-to-headers, which streaming respects, but a very long streaming generation (e.g. a multi-round tool agent) could in principle stall mid-stream with no per-read/idle timeout. Surfaced in the A#3 SSE-streaming review as an operational follow-up (not a correctness bug). Consider an idle/read timeout on the streamLoop brRead, or document the current behaviour. Low priority until a long-stream stall is actually observed.
close_reason: Shipped: acStreamIdleSecs config (default 60s) + AnthropicStreamTimeout error + timedRead wrapping each brRead in System.Timeout, threaded through streamLoop. Mid-stream stall now aborts (non-retryable) with bracketed responseClose instead of hanging. Merged to master (ae83793).

title: DRY: share request-building + retry between openStream and postMessages
description: openStream (Crucible.LLM.Anthropic.Stream) duplicates ~20 lines from postMessages (Crucible.LLM.Anthropic): the parseRequest+headers+RequestBodyLBS request builder, the recovering retry policy with the AnthropicError handler, and the maxBackoffMicros=30000000 constant. Accepted tradeoff during A#3 (kept the text path untouched + streaming self-contained). Now that openStream/postMessages/anthropicComplete/converseOnce all share the pattern, extract a buildMessagesRequest :: AnthropicConfig -> Value -> IO Request and a shared retry policy/constant into Anthropic.hs and have both call them. Behaviour-preserving refactor.

title: Explore CodeAct for crucible procedural memory
description: CodeAct (Wang et al., UIUC/MIT, ICML 2024, arXiv 2402.01030) uses executable Python as the agent action space. A stateful interpreter persists across turns; prior code actions accumulate as reusable procedural artifacts. Up to 20% higher success across 17 LLMs. Currently out of scope for crucible (action space is typed tool calls, no code interpreter). Research questions: could a sandboxed interpreter (Python via subprocess, or a Haskell eval) be exposed as a Tool in runToolAgentN? Would CodeAct-style code accumulation complement the SkillStore (crucible-6cl) — e.g. verified code snippets promoted to Procedural MemoryItems? What are the sandbox/security constraints? Is there a lighter version (shell tool + state file) that gives most of the benefit? Output: a design note or decision to defer.

title: Explore GraphRAG for crucible semantic memory
description: GraphRAG (Microsoft, 2024) extends flat vector RAG to a knowledge graph: GPT-4 extracts entities and relationships from documents, builds a relational store with Leiden community detection for hierarchy, and enables multi-hop reasoning across entities. Currently out of scope for crucible (no graph data structure exists) but worth understanding before designing the semantic memory layer. Research questions: what would a Graph module look like alongside Memory? Is there a lightweight Haskell graph store (e.g. algebraic-graphs, fgl) that fits the effect style? When does graph structure outperform flat cosine retrieval enough to justify the complexity? Does crucible's use case (skill/agent memory vs document corpora) even benefit from entity graphs? Output: a design note or decision to defer.

title: Persistent instruction store (LangMem pattern)
description: LangMem stores procedural memory as live system prompt updates: algorithms analyse session trajectories and rewrite the agent's instructions, which then persist as the new default behaviour. Crucible has improveSkill in Crucible.Skill.Improve (reflective prompt improvement) but the improved Instruction is returned to the caller, not persisted. Required: (1) improveInstruction :: (LLM :> es, Memory :> es) => [MemoryItem] -> Instruction -> Eff es Instruction — analyses recent episodic/procedural memories and proposes instruction refinements; (2) a persisted Instruction store (JSONL or single JSON file, read at session start, written after improvement runs) so the refined instruction survives across sessions without the caller re-supplying it. This closes the loop between consolidation and the skill's operating parameters.

title: Typed Workflow type and AWM-style episodic-to-procedural consolidation
description: AWM (Wang et al., ICML 2025) compresses episodic traces into typed {goal, routine} workflow objects stored and retrieved by goal similarity. Crucible's consolidate produces free-form MemoryItems with ByConsolidation provenance — there is no typed Workflow. Required: (1) data Workflow = Workflow { goal :: Text, steps :: [Text] } with a JSONCodec; (2) consolidate variant that produces Workflow items (Procedural kind) from episodic clusters; (3) recallAs WorkflowCodec for retrieval by cosine on the goal field; (4) withWorkflow :: (Memory :> es, Embed :> es) => Text -> Skill i o -> Skill i o — recalls the best-fit workflow by goal similarity and prepends its steps to the skill preamble. Depends on embedding-at-write and crucible-try.

title: Runtime SkillStore with embedding retrieval and verifyAndStore
description: Voyager maintains an ever-growing library of verified executable routines indexed by embedding. Crucible's Skill type is static (Haskell definitions at compile time). Required: a SkillStore backed by Procedural MemoryItems — each item's content is the skill's serialized name + description; retrieval uses embed on the task goal + cosine over stored descriptions to return top-k candidate skill names. Also needed: verifyAndStore :: Skill i o -> TestSuite i o -> Eff es (Maybe MemoryId) — runs testSkill on the candidate and only remembers it if it passes, preventing unverified skills from polluting the store. Depends on embedding-at-write.

title: selectTool by embedding similarity (HuggingGPT pattern)
description: HuggingGPT routes subtasks to tools by reading a semantic store of tool capability descriptions. Crucible has the Tool type (name, description, inputSchema) and the Embed effect but no semantic routing. Required: selectTool :: (Embed :> es) => [Tool] -> Text -> Eff es [Tool] — embeds the task description and each tool's description, ranks by cosine similarity, returns top-k tools. This lets large tool registries be narrowed to relevant candidates before being injected into the prompt, avoiding context bloat as tool counts grow.

title: Embedding-based clustering before consolidation
description: The consolidationSkill picks items to merge/supersede freely, without grouping related episodes first. Generative Agents clusters by embedding similarity before reflection, so related episodes distil together rather than being mixed arbitrarily. Gap: before calling consolidationSkill, group [MemoryItem] by cosine similarity (threshold ~0.8) and run consolidation per cluster. Requires embedding-at-write (see related issue). This makes consolidation produce coherent semantic facts rather than arbitrary cross-topic merges.

title: Research: lint as Eval cases
description: Follow-on to crucible-1z4. Structural lint (orphans, broken links, sparse pages) as pure functions over the page set; semantic lint (contradiction between pages, staleness) via the judge vote machinery, returning the Eval Report type so runEval applies. See llm-wiki research rec 5.
close_reason: Shipped Crucible.Research.Lint: pure structural lint (orphans/brokenLinks/sparsePages) + judge-based contradiction (over pairs) and staleness (vs current facts), unified by lintWiki/LintOpts, returning typed Findings. Merged to master, suite green (11 checks), docs/research.md section, live demo verified (3 orphans/1 sparse/1 contradiction, no false-positive staleness after a rubric fix the live run surfaced). Completes the Research arc (foundation + grounded writes + tools + lint).

title: Concurrent/async subagent spawn interpreter
description: Follow-on to crucible-pch. A concurrent spawn interpreter (parallel siblings) as an alternative to runAgents, without changing the Agents effect. Anthropic shipped synchronous and called async an open problem; Cognition's failure mode is uncoordinated parallel siblings, so this is lower priority and needs a coordination story.
close_reason: Shipped concurrent spawn: runAgents budget is now an atomic compare-and-set (race-free, sequential behavior unchanged) + spawnAll concurrent fan-out via effectful Concurrent (results in input order, one shared atomic budget across the tree, siblings share no state, failures are values). No Agents effect change. Merged to master, suite green (2 concurrency checks), docs/subagents.md updated, live demo verified (3 parallel weather workers). Completes the 4 spawn follow-ons (gated, ledger, nested, concurrent).

A Case/Expectation wall-clock predicate would only be meaningful under live interpreters and is noise under cassettes/scripted runs; the review ranks it last and recommends low priority. Filed for completeness; consider gating on the CallLog work (timing data source) and an explicit live-only marker in the report. See the BAML review note item 7.

title: Main typed-fn demo prints decode error on live runs
description: In app/Main.hs the typed-fn (llmFn classify) demo prints 'typed fn decode error: expected string, got object' on a successful live API call, because the model replies with surrounding prose/structure rather than a bare word, so the str decoder fails. Pre-existing (predates A#4 usage capture), surfaced during the usage-capture final review. Demo-only cosmetic; fix by tightening the prompt to force a single bare word, or making the demo tolerant. Out of scope for usage capture.
